primary_diagnosis.name: Mitral regurgitation with acute decompensated left ventricular failure and early pulmonary oedema

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic mitral regurgitation imposes a progressive volume overload on the left atrium and left ventricle. Over time the ventricle dilates and its contractile reserve is exhausted, so it can no longer eject the regurgitant plus forward volume — left ventricular decompensation. Left atrial and pulmonary venous pressures rise, driving transudation of fluid into the alveoli (early pulmonary oedema). Clinically this produces exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea/cough, fatigue and dependent oedema. The regurgitant jet generates a loud apical pansystolic murmur radiating to the axilla; the volume-loaded ventricle produces a laterally displaced hyperdynamic apex and an S3 gallop; rapid ventricular filling softens S1. Bibasal crackles and hypoxia mark the pulmonary congestion. The acute two-week deterioration represents tipping from compensated chronic MR into decompensated heart failure, which requires diuresis, echocardiographic quantification of the lesion and LV function, and surgical/structural valve assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated heart failure syndrome (cause to be defined)
differential.evidence_basis: Exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, fatigue and ankle oedema over months with acute worsening is a classic congestive picture.
Progressive breathlessness and night cough can be respiratory; must be actively distinguished from cardiac causes.

differential.diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia / lower respiratory tract infection
differential.evidence_basis: Breathlessness, cough and hypoxia — but no fever, purulent sputum or focal onset described.
Can precipitate acute LV failure; must be screened even though chest pain and palpitations are denied.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor should hold a cardiac (congestive) syndrome as leading while keeping primary respiratory disease and ACS in view; it cannot yet name the valve lesion.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated left ventricular failure (valvular cause most likely)
differential.evidence_basis: Orthopnoea + PND + dependent oedema + rest dyspnoea is a congestive syndrome; the trajectory fits volume overload rather than airways disease.
Less likely once orthopnoea and PND dominate and there is no wheeze or smoking-driven airflow history, but not formally excluded without examination/spirometry.
Silent ischaemia can drive decompensation; ECG and troponin are needed to exclude — impossible via telehealth.
Fatigue and exertional dyspnoea can be aggravated by anaemia; a bloods screen is warranted.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct move is to recognise a decompensated heart-failure syndrome and escalate for in-person work-up, NOT to commit to a respiratory diagnosis or attempt to name the valve lesion remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Mitral regurgitation causing decompensated LV failure
differential.evidence_basis: Auscultation (loud apical pansystolic murmur radiating to axilla, soft S1, S3), displaced hyperdynamic apex, bibasal crackles and hypoxia, confirmed by echocardiography showing a regurgitant jet — all requiring in-person assessment/investigation.
Ejection systolic murmur at the base radiating to the carotids, not a pansystolic apical murmur radiating to the axilla.
Would give a low-pitched mid-diastolic rumble and a tapping (not hyperdynamic) apex.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive valve lesion identification is an in-person/echocardiographic conclusion — sealed here as the answer key and NOT achievable by the telehealth AI Doctor.

differential.diagnosis: Mitral regurgitation with acute decompensated left ventricular failure and early pulmonary oedema
differential.evidence_basis: Full constellation on examination and pending echocardiography; requires urgent diuresis, valve quantification and cardiology/surgical assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor passes by recognising the decompensated heart-failure syndrome and escalating; it is NOT expected to independently confirm mitral regurgitation.

red_flags.description: Orthopnoea (three-pillow) with paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea and nocturnal cough — pulmonary congestion / decompensation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orthopnoea and PND indicate raised left-heart filling pressures and pulmonary venous congestion — hallmarks of decompensating left ventricular failure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to frank pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure if congestion is not treated urgently.

red_flags.description: Dyspnoea at rest with resting tachypnoea and hypoxia (SpO2 94%)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Breathlessness at rest with hypoxia signals significant pulmonary congestion and limited physiological reserve — a same-day hospital problem.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage to routine review while the patient tips into flash pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure.

red_flags.description: Loud apical pansystolic murmur radiating to the axilla, soft S1, S3 gallop, laterally displaced hyperdynamic apex (clinician auscultation/palpation only)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The pathognomonic examination signature of significant mitral regurgitation with a volume-loaded, decompensating left ventricle.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The structural valve cause is missed and the patient is mislabelled with a respiratory illness.

red_flags.description: Fine bibasal inspiratory crackles — early pulmonary oedema (clinician auscultation only)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms alveolar/interstitial congestion from raised left-heart pressures; cannot be auscultated over telehealth.

red_flags.description: Risk of flash pulmonary oedema — sudden severe breathlessness at rest and pink frothy sputum
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema is immediately life-threatening and mandates emergency ambulance transfer.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rapid respiratory failure and death if 000 escalation advice is not given up front.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing breathlessness, night cough and hypoxia to COPD/asthma or a chest infection and treating with salbutamol, antibiotics or steroids — missing the cardiac cause and delaying diuresis.
Not recognising that new three-pillow orthopnoea and PND are specific markers of left-heart decompensation, and under-triaging a genuinely unwell patient.

Trying to confirm the valve lesion or grade severity remotely; the diagnosis depends on auscultation, apex palpation, oximetry, ECG, chest X-ray and echocardiography — none available via chat. The correct output is escalation, not a remote diagnosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from denied chest pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured because the patient denies chest pain/palpitations and downgrading urgency, when decompensated heart failure and even silent ischaemia can present exactly this way.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Class III-IV — breathless on minimal exertion and at rest
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Advanced symptomatic heart failure warranting urgent assessment and treatment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of the apical pansystolic murmur, soft S1 and S3 gallop
Palpation of the laterally displaced hyperdynamic apex beat
Measured blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and SpO2 without a home device
12-lead ECG and troponin to exclude acute coronary syndrome
Chest X-ray, BNP/NT-proBNP and echocardiography to confirm congestion, LV function and the valve lesion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a decompensated heart-failure syndrome and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department) for oximetry, ECG, chest X-ray, bloods/BNP and echocardiography, with intravenous diuresis and cardiology referral. Diuretic therapy cannot be safely initiated remotely. Give explicit 000 safety-netting for flash pulmonary oedema.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main risk is UNDER-action: mislabelling this as a chest infection or COPD flare and treating with antibiotics/steroids/salbutamol, or under-triaging to a routine review, instead of escalating a decompensating patient for in-person work-up.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NHFA/CSANZ Guidelines for the Prevention, Detection and Management of Heart Failure in Australia 2018
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Patients with new or decompensated heart failure and congestion require prompt assessment, loop diuretic therapy titrated to congestion, echocardiography to define aetiology and LV function, and referral for management of the underlying cause including valvular disease.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC/EACTS Guidelines for the management of valvular heart disease
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Symptomatic severe primary mitral regurgitation is an indication for surgical/structural intervention assessment; onset of heart-failure symptoms is a trigger for referral.
